alternatives.name: In-Memory Store with localStorage Persistence
alternatives.description: Extend the existing in-memory state management (e.g., React context or Zustand/Redux store) to serialize all three entities (Expense, Category, RecurringTemplate) to localStorage on every mutation. On app boot, hydrate state from localStorage. RecurringTemplate occurrences are computed on-the-fly in a pure function at render time — no separate DB query needed.
tradeoffs: Minimal blast radius: only the store layer and 2–3 existing mutation hooks need updating (addExpense, deleteExpense, plus new CRUD for RecurringTemplate).
Zero new runtime dependencies — localStorage is a browser native API.
localStorage is capped at ~5 MB per origin; large expense histories (thousands of records) may hit this limit over time.
No cross-tab or cross-device sync; data is siloed to a single browser profile.
Serialization/deserialization on every write adds a small synchronous cost (~1–5 ms for typical datasets), but is imperceptible for the expected data volume.

Deleting a RecurringTemplate is safe by design: Expense records are stored independently, so soft-delete of the template leaves historical Expense rows untouched — satisfying the Data Integrity constraint with no extra logic.
Occurrence computation is a pure JS function (no I/O), making Dashboard load time purely CPU-bound and sub-millisecond for weekly/monthly/yearly schedules.

alternatives.name: Client-Side SQLite via sql.js (WASM)
alternatives.description: Introduce sql.js (SQLite compiled to WebAssembly) as a client-side relational store. Define three tables mirroring the data entities: expenses, categories, recurring_templates. The DB file is serialized to localStorage or IndexedDB on each write. Occurrence generation uses a SQL query or a thin JS layer on top of the template rows.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: sql.js (~1.5 MB WASM bundle), increasing initial load size noticeably — requires lazy loading or a web worker to avoid blocking the main thread.
Enables proper relational constraints (FK from expenses to recurring_templates) and SQL-based querying, which future features (forecasting, bulk ops) would benefit from.
Requires schema migration strategy from day one (even if just a version flag in localStorage) — adds ~1 new utility module.
Affects 4–6 modules: data access layer, store initialization, all three entity CRUD services, and the build config (WASM asset handling).
Deleting a RecurringTemplate with a FK constraint naturally prevents accidental cascade deletes if ON DELETE RESTRICT is set; historical Expense rows are preserved automatically.
WASM initialization adds ~100–300 ms cold-start latency on first load; subsequent loads from cached WASM are fast.
Overkill for the current feature scope (3 entities, desktop-only, no sync) but provides a clear upgrade path.

alternatives.name: IndexedDB via Dexie.js
alternatives.description: Replace or augment the current persistence layer with Dexie.js, a lightweight IndexedDB wrapper. Define three object stores (expenses, categories, recurringTemplates) with indexes on relevant fields (e.g., templateId on expenses, nextOccurrence on recurringTemplates). Occurrence generation remains a JS computation layer on top of fetched template records.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: Dexie.js (~22 KB gzipped) — negligible bundle impact compared to sql.js.
IndexedDB has no practical storage cap for typical personal finance data (quota is origin-based, usually GBs), solving the localStorage size concern.
All DB operations are async; requires converting any synchronous store reads to async/await, touching all 3 entity service modules and their consuming components (~8–12 files).
Dexie's versioned schema system provides a built-in migration path (db.version(2).stores(...).upgrade(...)), which is production-safe.
Deleting a RecurringTemplate requires an explicit application-level check (not a DB constraint) to avoid touching Expense records — a small guard in the delete service suffices.
Dashboard load performance: async IndexedDB reads for RecurringTemplate rows add ~5–20 ms (well within imperceptible threshold); occurrence computation remains pure JS.
Dexie LiveQuery enables reactive UI updates when template data changes, aligning well with React's rendering model.
Affects ~10–14 files: Dexie schema definition (1 new file), 3 entity service modules rewritten, ~6–8 component hooks updated to handle async data.

alternatives.name: Supabase (PostgreSQL) Backend
alternatives.description: Introduce Supabase as a hosted PostgreSQL backend with a JS client. All three entities are stored as server-side tables with proper relational integrity. The Supabase client handles auth, real-time subscriptions, and row-level security. Occurrence generation can be done either client-side (JS) or via a Postgres function/view.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency: @supabase/supabase-js (~40 KB gzipped), plus a Supabase project (external service dependency).

Introduces network latency on every read/write — Dashboard load now depends on a remote API call (~50–200 ms), which may violate the 'no perceptible delay' performance constraint without aggressive caching or optimistic UI.
Requires authentication/user identity to scope data per user — a significant scope expansion not implied by the current desktop-only, single-user context.
Enables cross-device sync and data durability beyond the browser, which is valuable but out of scope for this enhancement.

Highest blast radius: affects all 3 entity services, all data-fetching hooks, environment config (API keys), CI/CD (secrets management), and potentially adds a loading/error state to every data-dependent screen (~15–20 files).
Supabase RLS policies can enforce that deleting a RecurringTemplate never cascades to Expense rows — data integrity is handled at the DB level.
Overkill for a desktop-scoped, single-user app in this version; introduces operational overhead (Supabase project management, API key rotation, network failure handling).
Best justified only if cross-device sync or multi-user support is on the near-term roadmap.

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency minimal change to full backend integration, giving the Architecture Writer a clear cost/benefit gradient. Alt-1 (localStorage) is the required "minimal change" option — it touches the fewest files and introduces no new dependencies, making it the lowest-risk path for a brownfield desktop app. Alt-2 (sql.js) and Alt-3 (Dexie.js) represent the "structured client-side" middle ground; Dexie was preferred over sql.js as a separate option because it has a dramatically smaller bundle footprint and a native async API that fits React patterns better, while sql.js is included for teams that specifically want SQL semantics. Alt-4 (Supabase) is included because it was explicitly named in the gap description and must be evaluated, even though its blast radius and network-latency implications make it a poor fit for the current scope. Excluded alternatives: raw IndexedDB without a wrapper (too verbose, no migration support), Firebase/Firestore (same concerns as Supabase but with a proprietary query model), and PouchDB (adds CouchDB sync complexity irrelevant to a single-user desktop app).
structured.recommendation: Alt-3 (Dexie.js / IndexedDB) is the recommended choice. It eliminates the localStorage 5 MB storage ceiling that will eventually bite a personal finance app, adds only one small dependency (~22 KB), provides a first-class migration system for future schema changes, and keeps all data client-side — matching the desktop-scoped, single-user nature of CashPulse. The async overhead is well within the performance constraint (~5–20 ms), and Dexie's LiveQuery integrates cleanly with React. The medium blast radius (~10–14 files) is justified by the long-term durability of the solution. Alt-1 is the right choice only if the team wants to ship the fastest possible spike with zero risk, accepting that a storage-limit refactor will be needed later.

alternatives.name: CSS Modules (Minimal Change — Extend Existing Pattern)
alternatives.description: Adopt CSS Modules as the canonical styling methodology for all new recurring-transaction UI (recurrence config slide-down, Manage Recurring drawer, recurring dashboard card, recurrence toggle). Each new component gets a co-located `.module.css` file. If CashPulse already uses CSS Modules, this is zero-migration; if it uses plain CSS, the change is additive and scoped only to new files. Animation tokens are expressed as CSS custom properties in a shared `tokens.css` file, and `prefers-reduced-motion` is handled via a media query block in each module.
tradeoffs: Zero new runtime dependencies if the bundler (Vite/CRA/Next.js) already supports CSS Modules — which all common React bundlers do out of the box
Scoped class names eliminate collision risk with existing global styles
No JavaScript-in-CSS overhead; styles are extracted at build time, keeping Dashboard load performance optimal
Verbose for dynamic styles (e.g., toggling recurrence config visibility requires className conditionals or a utility like `clsx` — 1 lightweight dependency)
Sharing design tokens between JS and CSS requires CSS custom properties or a separate token file; no automatic type-safety on token names

Affects only the ~6 new component files (RecurrenceToggle, RecurrenceConfig, ManageRecurringDrawer, RecurringDashboardCard, RecurrenceForm, RecurrenceSettingsSection) — blast radius is strictly additive

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS (Utility-First — Additive Install)
alternatives.description: Install Tailwind CSS and apply utility classes directly in JSX for all new recurring-transaction components. Existing components are untouched unless the team opts to migrate them incrementally. Animation tokens map to Tailwind's `theme.extend` config (duration, easing). The `prefers-reduced-motion` constraint is satisfied via Tailwind's built-in `motion-reduce:` variant. The recurrence config slide-down and drawer slide-in use `transition-all` utilities combined with conditional class application.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new dev dependencies: `tailwindcss` and `postcss` (plus `autoprefixer`); requires a `tailwind.config.js` and PostCSS config — 3–4 new config files
If the existing app does NOT use Tailwind, introducing it mid-project creates a dual-methodology codebase (existing CSS + Tailwind), increasing cognitive overhead for the team
If the existing app already uses Tailwind, this is the zero-friction choice and blast radius is near-zero
Excellent performance: PurgeCSS/content scanning ensures only used utilities ship; no runtime style computation
Accessibility utilities (e.g., `sr-only`, `focus-visible`) are built-in, directly supporting the keyboard/screen-reader constraint for the recurrence toggle
Rapid prototyping of the drawer, card, and form config panel without context-switching to separate CSS files
Class strings on complex components (drawer, form) can become long and hard to review in PRs
Affects 6 new component files + 3–4 config files; existing components untouched

alternatives.name: styled-components (CSS-in-JS — Runtime Styling)
alternatives.description: Use styled-components to author all new recurring-transaction UI as styled React components. Dynamic styles (e.g., the recurrence config slide-down height, drawer open/closed state) are expressed as props-driven interpolations. Animation tokens are JS constants imported directly into styled definitions. The `prefers-reduced-motion` media query is embedded inline via the `css` helper. Focus trapping for the drawer uses a library like `focus-trap-react`.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 significant runtime dependency (`styled-components` ~12 kB gzip) that executes style injection at runtime — introduces a small but measurable JS parse/execution cost on Dashboard load, which conflicts with the performance soft constraint
Dynamic prop-driven styles are ergonomic and eliminate the need for `clsx` conditionals, making the recurrence toggle and config panel state management cleaner
Full TypeScript support for theme tokens via `DefaultTheme` augmentation — token names are type-safe
Server-side rendering requires `ServerStyleSheet` setup; if CashPulse is SSR/Next.js, this adds non-trivial configuration
If the existing codebase does not use CSS-in-JS, this introduces a third styling paradigm alongside whatever exists — highest cognitive overhead of all options
Styled component display names aid debugging in React DevTools
Affects 6 new component files + potential theme provider wrapping at app root (1–2 existing files touched if a ThemeProvider must be added)
styled-components v6 dropped IE11 support and changed the hashing algorithm — verify version compatibility with existing deps

alternatives.name: vanilla-extract (Zero-Runtime CSS-in-TypeScript)
alternatives.description: Use vanilla-extract to define styles in `.css.ts` files that are statically extracted at build time. All new recurring-transaction components (toggle, config panel, drawer, dashboard card) get co-located `.css.ts` files. Animation tokens are defined in a shared `tokens.css.ts` contract, giving full TypeScript type-safety on token names. Dynamic variants (open/closed drawer, toggled recurrence config) use vanilla-extract's `styleVariants` or `recipe` API. The `prefers-reduced-motion` query is expressed via the `@layer` and `@media` APIs in the `.css.ts` file.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new dependencies: `@vanilla-extract/css` and a bundler plugin (`@vanilla-extract/vite-plugin` or equivalent) — requires bundler config change (1 existing config file modified)
Zero runtime overhead: all styles extracted to static CSS at build time, fully satisfying the Dashboard performance constraint
Full TypeScript type-safety on class names and token references — catches token typos at compile time, unlike CSS Modules
Steeper learning curve than CSS Modules; the `.css.ts` file pattern is unfamiliar to developers who haven't used vanilla-extract before
`styleVariants` / `recipe` API elegantly handles the recurrence config slide-down and drawer open/closed states without `clsx` conditionals
Smaller ecosystem and community than Tailwind or styled-components; fewer Stack Overflow answers and third-party integrations
Affects 6 new component files + 1 shared token file + 1 bundler config file; no existing component files touched

structured.recommendation: alt-css-modules — If CashPulse already uses CSS Modules (common in React projects scaffolded with Vite or CRA), this is the zero-dependency, zero-migration path. It satisfies every soft constraint: build-time extraction keeps Dashboard load fast, `prefers-reduced-motion` is a native CSS media query, and focus-trap behavior is handled in JS independently of the styling layer. If the team is greenfield on styling methodology, `alt-tailwind` is the second choice — its built-in `motion-reduce:` variant and `sr-only` utility directly address the animation and accessibility constraints with minimal boilerplate. Avoid `alt-styled-components` given the explicit performance soft constraint on Dashboard load, as its runtime style injection adds unnecessary JS execution cost.
structured.rationale: These four alternatives cover the full spectrum from minimal-change (CSS Modules) to utility-first (Tailwind) to runtime CSS-in-JS (styled-components) to zero-runtime CSS-in-TypeScript (vanilla-extract). Each is implementable in a standard React brownfield project. Options excluded: plain global CSS (no scoping, high collision risk with existing styles), Emotion (same runtime cost concern as styled-components with less ecosystem momentum), Sass/SCSS modules (adds a preprocessor dependency with marginal benefit over CSS Modules given modern CSS custom properties handle tokens natively). The four chosen options represent meaningfully different tradeoff profiles across the axes that matter most for this project: Dashboard load performance, animation token integration, accessibility utility, and blast radius on the existing codebase.

alternatives.name: shadcn/ui (copy-into-repo model)
alternatives.description: Adopt shadcn/ui as the primary component library. Components are copied directly into the codebase (not installed as a black-box npm package), giving full ownership of markup and styles. Built on Radix UI primitives for accessibility and Tailwind CSS for styling. New recurring-transaction UI (toggle switch, drawer, config slide-down, dashboard card) is composed from shadcn primitives such as Switch, Sheet, Collapsible, and Card.
tradeoffs: Zero runtime bundle overhead from a library — only the components you copy are included
Radix UI primitives provide ARIA roles, keyboard navigation (Space/Enter toggle), and focus trapping out of the box — directly satisfies the accessibility constraint
Sheet (drawer) component ships with built-in focus trap and restores focus on close — no custom implementation needed
Tailwind-based styling integrates naturally with an existing Tailwind setup; if the project is NOT already on Tailwind, adds Tailwind as a new build dependency
Adds 2 new direct dependencies: radix-ui/* primitives (peer deps of shadcn components) and class-variance-authority for variant management
Copy-into-repo model means component updates require manual re-copy — no automatic upstream patches
Affects ~6 files/modules: Switch component, Sheet/Drawer component, Collapsible component, Card component, form field wrappers, and the shared component index
Animation tokens must be manually mapped to Tailwind keyframes to satisfy the Animation & Motion constraint — requires one tailwind.config.js edit

alternatives.name: Mantine v7
alternatives.description: Adopt Mantine as a full-featured component library. Mantine ships its own CSS-in-JS-free styling system (PostCSS + CSS modules in v7), a comprehensive set of accessible components, and a built-in animation/transition system. New UI elements map directly to Mantine's Switch, Drawer, Collapse, and Card components. Mantine's Transition component respects prefers-reduced-motion natively.
tradeoffs: Adds 3 new dependencies: @mantine/core, @mantine/hooks, and @mantine/dates (if date pickers are needed for recurrence end-date); PostCSS plugin required
Mantine's Transition component has built-in prefers-reduced-motion support — directly satisfies the Animation & Motion constraint with no extra work
Drawer component includes focus trapping and focus-return behavior out of the box
Switch component is keyboard accessible and announces state via ARIA — satisfies accessibility constraint
If the existing app uses a different styling system (e.g., Tailwind or plain CSS), Mantine's CSS layer may conflict or require specificity tuning — medium integration risk
Larger bundle footprint than shadcn/ui if tree-shaking is not configured correctly; requires explicit PostCSS setup
Affects ~8–10 files/modules: all new UI components plus any existing components that need theme-token alignment
Opinionated theming system may require migrating existing color/spacing tokens to Mantine's theme object — 1–2 days of alignment work

alternatives.name: Material UI (MUI) v5/v6
alternatives.description: Adopt MUI as the component library. MUI is the most widely adopted React component library, with a comprehensive set of accessible components, a mature theming system, and strong TypeScript support. New recurring-transaction UI maps to MUI's Switch, Drawer, Collapse, and Card components.
tradeoffs: Adds 3 new dependencies: @mui/material, @emotion/react, @emotion/styled (Emotion is MUI's default styling engine) — or @mui/material + @mui/system if using the sx prop only
Emotion runtime adds ~7–10 KB gzipped to the bundle; if the project already uses styled-components, a compatibility shim is needed
MUI Drawer includes focus trapping (via react-aria under the hood) and focus-return on close
Switch and all form controls are WCAG 2.1 AA compliant with proper ARIA attributes
Material Design aesthetic may clash with an existing custom design system — requires theme overrides for colors, typography, and border-radius to match CashPulse's visual language
MUI's animation system uses CSS transitions internally; prefers-reduced-motion must be handled via a custom GlobalStyles override or theme.transitions.create with a conditional duration
Affects ~10–14 files/modules: all new components, existing components that share layout primitives, theme configuration, and global style overrides
Heaviest integration cost of all alternatives if the existing app is not already on MUI — estimated 2–4 days of theme alignment

alternatives.name: Custom components on Radix UI primitives (minimal change)
alternatives.description: Build only the required new components (recurrence toggle, config panel, manage-recurring drawer, dashboard card) directly on top of Radix UI primitives without adopting any full component library. Existing components remain untouched. This is the minimal-blast-radius option: only the four new UI surfaces are added, styled with whatever CSS approach the project already uses.
tradeoffs: Adds only 3 targeted Radix UI packages: @radix-ui/react-switch, @radix-ui/react-dialog (for the drawer/focus trap), and @radix-ui/react-collapsible (for the config slide-down) — no full library adoption
Zero impact on existing components — blast radius is strictly limited to the 4 new component files and their imports
Radix primitives are unstyled — all visual styling must be written from scratch using the project's existing CSS approach, which is consistent but requires more authoring effort
Focus trapping and focus-return are handled by Radix Dialog primitive natively — satisfies the accessibility constraint
Switch primitive is keyboard accessible (Space/Enter) and emits correct ARIA — satisfies the accessibility constraint
Animation (slide-down, slide-in) must be implemented manually using CSS keyframes or the project's existing animation tokens — requires careful implementation to honor prefers-reduced-motion
Affects only ~4–5 files/modules: RecurrenceToggle, RecurrenceConfigPanel, ManageRecurringDrawer, UpcomingRecurringCard, and the shared component index
No design-system consistency guarantee beyond what the developer manually enforces — higher long-term maintenance burden if more components are added later

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from minimal-change (custom on Radix) to full library adoption (MUI), covering the two most popular modern React component libraries (shadcn/ui and Mantine) in between. MUI was included because it is the most widely known option in the gap description, but its high blast radius and Emotion dependency make it a poor fit for a brownfield project with an existing styling system. Alternatives like Chakra UI and Ant Design were excluded: Chakra v3 is undergoing a major rewrite with uncertain stability, and Ant Design's enterprise-focused aesthetic and large bundle size are mismatched with a personal finance dashboard. The four selected alternatives all satisfy the hard accessibility requirements (keyboard-accessible switch, focus-trapping drawer) either natively or with minimal custom work, and all are implementable without replacing the existing React stack.
structured.recommendation: alt-shadcn-ui — shadcn/ui offers the best balance for this brownfield project. Its copy-into-repo model means zero library lock-in and minimal bundle impact, Radix UI primitives satisfy every accessibility constraint out of the box (keyboard toggle, focus trap, focus return), and Tailwind-based styling is easy to align with existing animation tokens. The blast radius is low (≈6 files), and the four new UI surfaces (toggle, config panel, drawer, dashboard card) map 1:1 to existing shadcn primitives. If the project is not already on Tailwind, alt-custom-headless becomes the preferred fallback, as it achieves the same Radix-primitive accessibility guarantees with even fewer new dependencies and no styling-system change.

alternatives.name: Repository Pattern with Typed Repositories per Entity
alternatives.description: Introduce a dedicated repository class for each data entity (ExpenseRepository, CategoryRepository, RecurringTemplateRepository). Each repository encapsulates all query logic, CRUD operations, and relationship traversal for its entity. The RecurringTemplateRepository owns the logic for fetching templates with their linked Expense records and computing upcoming occurrences. Repositories are injected into service/hook layers and never called directly from UI components. This is the most structured alternative and cleanly separates data access from business logic (e.g., occurrence computation lives in a RecurrenceService, not in the repository).
tradeoffs: Adds 3 new repository files + 1 RecurrenceService file (4 new modules total)
Requires refactoring existing direct data calls in UI components to route through repositories — estimated 5–8 component files affected
Highest upfront boilerplate, but yields the cleanest boundary for future auto-generation feature (scope-excluded now, likely added later)
Occurrence computation is fully isolated in RecurrenceService, making it independently testable and cacheable — directly addresses the Dashboard performance constraint
Deleting a RecurringTemplate is a single repository method that can enforce the data integrity constraint (no cascade delete to past Expense records) in one place
Adds 0 new external dependencies if implemented in plain TypeScript classes

alternatives.name: Active Record via ORM (e.g., Prisma or Drizzle model methods)
alternatives.description: Leverage an ORM that provides Active Record-style model methods directly on entity objects (e.g., Prisma Client's generated model API or Drizzle's table-scoped query builder). Each entity (Expense, Category, RecurringTemplate) is accessed via the ORM client directly in service hooks or server actions. Relationships (e.g., RecurringTemplate → Expense) are expressed as ORM-level relations and resolved via include/join clauses at the call site. No separate repository abstraction layer is introduced.
tradeoffs: Adds 1–2 new dependencies (ORM package + DB driver) if not already present in the stack — significant if brownfield stack is currently using raw storage (localStorage, IndexedDB, or a lightweight client-side store)
Reduces boilerplate vs. Repository pattern — no repository classes needed, query logic lives in hooks/actions directly
Occurrence computation for the Dashboard card still needs a dedicated utility function; without a service layer, this logic risks leaking into components
Schema migrations become an explicit concern — adding RecurringTemplate (entity-003) and the foreign key to Expense (entity-001) requires a migration step
Tight coupling of query logic to ORM API makes it harder to swap storage backends later (relevant if the app currently uses client-side storage)
Blast radius: 3–5 existing data-fetching hooks modified + 1 new schema migration file + 1 new occurrence utility

alternatives.name: Composable Query Builder Hooks (minimal change)
alternatives.description: The minimal-change alternative. Extend the existing data access pattern (assumed to be custom hooks or simple fetch utilities) by adding composable query builder hooks for the new RecurringTemplate entity and augmenting the existing Expense hooks. A useRecurringTemplates() hook handles CRUD for entity-003. A useUpcomingOccurrences() hook accepts a list of templates and returns computed upcoming dates — this is a pure computation hook with no I/O, keeping Dashboard load fast. The existing useExpenses() hook is extended with an optional templateId filter to support the data integrity constraint (past expenses linked to a deleted template are preserved by nullifying the FK, not cascading). No new abstraction layer or ORM is introduced.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new hook files (useRecurringTemplates, useUpcomingOccurrences) and modifies 1 existing hook (useExpenses) — lowest blast radius of all alternatives
Zero new external dependencies if the existing stack already has a data layer (e.g., React Query + fetch, Zustand, or similar)
Occurrence computation logic lives in a hook rather than a dedicated service — acceptable for the current scope (no auto-generation), but will need extraction if auto-generation is added later
Query logic remains distributed across hooks rather than centralized — harder to enforce data integrity rules consistently as the feature grows
Easiest to implement without disrupting the existing Add Expense form flow (usability constraint), since new hooks are purely additive
Affected files: ~3 hook files + ~2 component files (Dashboard card, Manage Recurring drawer)

alternatives.name: Repository Pattern + In-Memory Occurrence Cache
alternatives.description: Same repository structure as alt-1, but adds a lightweight in-memory cache (e.g., a Map keyed by templateId + date window) specifically for the occurrence computation results in RecurrenceService. On Dashboard load, the cache is checked first; if warm, upcoming occurrences are returned synchronously with zero latency. The cache is invalidated when a RecurringTemplate is created, updated, or deleted. This directly targets the Dashboard performance soft constraint without introducing a full server-side caching layer. The cache lives in a singleton service module and is scoped to the browser session.
tradeoffs: Adds 4 new modules (3 repositories + 1 RecurrenceService with cache) — same as alt-1 plus cache logic within RecurrenceService
Cache invalidation logic adds ~30–50 lines to RecurrenceService and must be called from all three mutation paths (create/update/delete template) — 3 additional call sites to maintain
Eliminates perceptible delay on Dashboard re-renders after initial load, directly satisfying the performance soft constraint
In-memory cache is lost on page refresh — acceptable since occurrence computation is fast on cold load (weekly/monthly/yearly math is O(n) on template count, not a DB query)

Most future-proof option: repository boundaries make it straightforward to add auto-generation (deferred feature) and the cache layer can be promoted to a server cache (Redis, etc.) without changing the repository interface
Blast radius: same as alt-1 (~5–8 component files) + 1 cache module within RecurrenceService

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from minimal-change (alt-3) to fully structured with performance optimization (alt-4), all implementable without leaving the brownfield JavaScript/TypeScript ecosystem. Active Record via ORM (alt-2) was included because it is a common industry choice, but flagged as high blast radius due to the likely mismatch with a client-side storage model typical of a desktop-scoped personal finance app. A raw SQL / stored-procedure approach was excluded because the app description and scope (client-side dashboard, no mention of a backend DB) make it inapplicable. A full CQRS/event-sourcing pattern was excluded as disproportionate to the feature scope (3 entities, 8 features). The four selected alternatives each satisfy the data integrity constraint (past expenses preserved on template deletion) and the performance constraint (occurrence computation isolated from I/O), differing primarily in how much structure and future-proofing they introduce.
structured.recommendation: alt-3-query-builder-hooks for teams prioritizing delivery speed with the current scope, or alt-4-repository-with-in-memory-cache if the team anticipates the auto-generation feature being added within 1–2 sprints. Alt-3 minimizes blast radius and keeps the existing Add Expense form flow untouched (directly satisfying the usability soft constraint), while alt-4 provides the cleanest foundation for the deferred auto-generation feature and explicitly solves the Dashboard performance constraint with a cache. Alt-1 is the right call if the team wants repository structure without the cache complexity. Alt-2 should only be chosen if an ORM is already present in the stack.

alternatives.name: In-Memory Memoization (Minimal Change)
alternatives.description: Cache computed upcoming occurrences for each RecurringTemplate in a plain JavaScript Map or object within the API layer (or frontend service layer). On each dashboard load, check if a cached result exists for a given template ID + current date window; if so, return it immediately. Invalidate the cache entry when a RecurringTemplate is created, updated, or deleted. No new infrastructure is required — this is a pure code-level addition to the existing service/utility that computes occurrences.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies — uses native JS Map/object
Cache is process-scoped: resets on every server restart or page reload (acceptable for a desktop brownfield app with infrequent restarts)
Cache is not shared across tabs or users — each browser tab/session computes independently on first load
Invalidation logic must be co-located with RecurringTemplate mutation handlers (create/update/delete) — adds ~3–5 lines per mutation site
Memory footprint is negligible for typical user template counts (< 50 templates), but unbounded growth is possible if not capped
Affects ~2–3 files: the occurrence-computation utility, the dashboard data-fetching hook/service, and the RecurringTemplate mutation handlers

alternatives.name: HTTP Cache-Control Headers (Stale-While-Revalidate)
alternatives.description: Leverage standard HTTP caching on the API endpoint that returns upcoming occurrences for the dashboard card. Set Cache-Control: max-age=300, stale-while-revalidate=600 on the GET /recurring-templates/upcoming response. The browser (or a service worker) serves the stale cached response instantly while revalidating in the background. No server-side cache store is needed — the browser cache acts as the cache layer. Pair with an ETag or Last-Modified header derived from the latest RecurringTemplate updatedAt timestamp for precise invalidation.
tradeoffs: Zero new server-side dependencies — uses existing HTTP infrastructure
Works automatically across page reloads and browser tabs for the same origin
Cache lifetime is time-based (5 min max-age); a template update won't invalidate the cache until the next revalidation cycle unless the client explicitly refetches (e.g., after a mutation)
Requires the dashboard data-fetching layer to issue a cache-busting refetch after any RecurringTemplate mutation — adds ~2–4 lines per mutation handler
ETag computation requires reading the latest updatedAt from the DB on every request, adding a lightweight DB query
Does not help with the first load on a cold cache — occurrence computation still runs server-side on first request
Affects ~3–5 files: API route handler for upcoming occurrences, dashboard fetch hook, and RecurringTemplate mutation handlers

alternatives.name: Precomputed & Persisted Occurrences on Template Write
alternatives.description: Instead of computing upcoming occurrences at read time, compute and persist the next N occurrences (e.g., next 12) into a dedicated OccurrenceCache table (or a JSON column on RecurringTemplate) whenever a template is created or updated. The dashboard GET endpoint simply reads the pre-persisted rows — no computation at read time. Occurrences are regenerated on every template write. A lightweight scheduled job (or trigger) can refresh occurrences that have passed their date. This keeps the dashboard read path to a simple DB SELECT.
tradeoffs: Eliminates all runtime occurrence computation on the dashboard read path — dashboard load becomes a trivial DB read
Adds 1 new DB table or JSON column to RecurringTemplate (entity-003) — requires a schema migration
Write path becomes heavier: every template create/update must compute and persist N occurrences — adds ~10–20ms to write operations (acceptable)
Stale data risk: if the system clock or template config drifts, persisted occurrences may be slightly out of date until next write; mitigated by a nightly refresh job
Adds complexity to the delete flow: must also purge future persisted occurrences while preserving past expense records (aligns with the Data Integrity constraint)
Affects ~5–7 files: DB schema/migration, RecurringTemplate repository, occurrence computation utility, dashboard API route, and template mutation handlers (create/update/delete)

alternatives.name: Redis Cache with On-Demand Population
alternatives.description: Introduce Redis as a shared cache layer. On dashboard load, the API checks Redis for a cached list of upcoming occurrences keyed by userId + templateId. On a cache miss, compute occurrences, store the result in Redis with a TTL of 1 hour, and return. On any RecurringTemplate mutation (create/update/delete), explicitly delete the affected Redis key(s) to force recomputation on next read. This is the canonical cache-aside pattern.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new infrastructure dependency: Redis (or a managed equivalent like Upstash) — requires provisioning, connection management, and operational overhead
Adds 1 new npm dependency (e.g., ioredis or @upstash/redis) to the project
Cache is shared across all sessions and server instances — ideal if CashPulse ever scales to multi-user or multi-instance, but likely over-engineered for a single-user desktop app
Requires handling Redis connection failures gracefully (fallback to live computation) — adds error-handling branches in ~2–3 files
Explicit cache invalidation on mutations is required — same pattern as alt-1 but with network I/O to Redis instead of in-process Map operations
Highest operational complexity of all alternatives; justified only if the app is expected to scale beyond a single-user desktop context
Affects ~6–8 files: Redis client setup/config, dashboard API route, RecurringTemplate mutation handlers, occurrence computation utility, environment config, and deployment/infrastructure files

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-infrastructure to full external cache, covering the realistic solution space for a brownfield desktop app. Redis (alt-4) was included for completeness but is flagged as likely over-engineered given CashPulse's desktop-scoped, single-user context. A database-level precomputation approach (alt-3) was included because it directly eliminates read-time computation — the root cause of the performance constraint — at the cost of a schema migration. Pure service-worker caching was excluded because CashPulse is described as a desktop app without explicit PWA/service-worker infrastructure. CDN-level caching was excluded because the data is user-specific and not publicly cacheable. The recommendation favors alt-2 (HTTP Cache-Control with stale-while-revalidate) as the best balance: zero new dependencies, cross-tab effectiveness, browser-native, and directly addresses the dashboard load performance constraint with minimal blast radius. Alt-1 is the fallback if the API layer does not support header control.
structured.recommendation: alt-2 — HTTP Cache-Control with stale-while-revalidate is the recommended choice. It requires no new dependencies or infrastructure, leverages the browser's native cache (effective across page reloads), and directly satisfies the soft performance constraint that dashboard loads must not feel slow. The stale-while-revalidate pattern means users always see an instant response from cache while the background revalidation keeps data fresh. Pairing it with a cache-busting refetch after any RecurringTemplate mutation ensures correctness without complex invalidation logic. If the project later moves toward multi-user or server-rendered contexts, alt-3 (precomputed occurrences) becomes the stronger long-term choice as it eliminates computation from the read path entirely.

adrs.title: Use Dexie.js (IndexedDB) for Client-Side Data Persistence
adrs.decision: Adopt Dexie.js as the persistence layer for all CashPulse data entities (Expense, Category, RecurringTemplate). Define three object stores with indexes on categoryId, recurringTemplateId, nextOccurrence, and date fields. Use Dexie's versioned schema system for migrations.
adrs.rationale: CashPulse is a desktop-scoped, single-user app with no backend server. localStorage's 5 MB cap is a ticking time bomb for a personal finance app that accumulates expense records indefinitely. Dexie.js adds only ~22 KB gzipped, provides async IndexedDB access that integrates cleanly with React hooks, and includes a first-class migration system (db.version(N).stores(...).upgrade(...)) for the new RecurringTemplate entity. The async read overhead (~5–20 ms) is well within the 500 ms dashboard load budget. sql.js was rejected due to its 1.5 MB WASM bundle and cold-start latency. Supabase was rejected as it introduces network latency, auth requirements, and operational overhead disproportionate to a single-user desktop app. localStorage was rejected due to the storage ceiling and synchronous blocking writes.
alternatives: alt-1: In-Memory Store with localStorage Persistence — lowest blast radius but 5 MB storage cap will require future refactor
alt-2: Client-Side SQLite via sql.js — 1.5 MB WASM bundle, cold-start latency, overkill for 3 entities
alt-4: Supabase PostgreSQL — network latency, auth scope expansion, operational overhead for a single-user desktop app

adrs.title: Use Tailwind CSS for Styling Methodology
adrs.decision: Adopt Tailwind CSS as the canonical styling approach for all new recurring-transaction UI components and as the standard for the project going forward. Map CashPulse design tokens (colors, spacing, typography, radii, shadows, animation durations) to tailwind.config.js theme.extend entries. Use the motion-reduce: variant for prefers-reduced-motion compliance.
adrs.rationale: Tailwind provides utility-first styling with zero runtime overhead (PurgeCSS ensures only used utilities ship), built-in accessibility utilities (sr-only, focus-visible), and native prefers-reduced-motion support via the motion-reduce: variant — directly satisfying NFR-003 and NFR-005. The PRD's design tokens map cleanly to Tailwind's theme configuration. This choice also aligns with the shadcn/ui component library decision (ADR-003), which is built on Tailwind. CSS Modules were considered but lack built-in accessibility utilities and require more boilerplate for dynamic states. styled-components was rejected due to runtime style injection conflicting with the dashboard performance constraint.
alternatives: alt-css-modules: CSS Modules — zero dependencies but verbose for dynamic styles, no built-in a11y utilities
alt-styled-components: styled-components — runtime JS overhead conflicts with performance constraint
alt-vanilla-extract: vanilla-extract — excellent type-safety but steeper learning curve and smaller ecosystem

adrs.title: Use shadcn/ui (Radix UI Primitives) as Component Library
adrs.decision: Adopt shadcn/ui as the component library for all new UI surfaces. Copy Switch, Sheet (drawer), Collapsible, and Card primitives into the codebase. Leverage Radix UI's built-in ARIA roles, keyboard navigation, and focus trapping for the recurrence toggle, manage-recurring drawer, and config panel.
adrs.rationale: shadcn/ui's copy-into-repo model gives full ownership of markup and styles with zero runtime bundle overhead. Radix UI primitives provide WCAG 2.1 AA-compliant keyboard navigation (Space/Enter toggle on Switch), focus trapping (Sheet/Dialog), and focus-return on close — directly satisfying NFR-003 without custom implementation. The four new UI surfaces (RecurrenceToggle, RecurrenceConfigPanel, ManageRecurringDrawer, UpcomingRecurringCard) map 1:1 to existing shadcn primitives. The Tailwind-based styling integrates naturally with ADR-002. Mantine was rejected due to potential CSS layer conflicts and larger bundle. MUI was rejected due to Emotion runtime dependency and Material Design aesthetic mismatch with CashPulse's custom design language.
alternatives: alt-mantine: Mantine v7 — CSS layer conflicts with Tailwind, larger bundle, opinionated theming
alt-mui: MUI — Emotion runtime dependency, Material Design aesthetic clash, highest integration cost
alt-custom-headless: Custom on Radix primitives — viable fallback but more authoring effort without shadcn's pre-built compositions

adrs.title: Use Composable Query Builder Hooks for Data Access
adrs.decision: Extend the existing data access pattern with composable query builder hooks: useRecurringTemplates() for CRUD on RecurringTemplate, useUpcomingOccurrences() for pure computation of upcoming dates, and extend useExpenses() with an optional recurringTemplateId filter. No new abstraction layer (repository, ORM) is introduced.
adrs.rationale: This is the minimal-blast-radius option (~3 hook files + ~2 component files), keeping the existing Add Expense form flow completely untouched (satisfying NFR-002). Occurrence computation is isolated in a pure-computation hook with no I/O, keeping dashboard load fast. The data integrity constraint (NFR-004) is enforced by nullifying the recurringTemplateId FK on linked expenses when a template is deleted, rather than cascading. The repository pattern (alt-1/alt-4) was considered but introduces 4+ new modules and requires refactoring existing direct data calls — disproportionate to the current 3-entity scope. An ORM-based active record pattern was rejected as it would introduce a server-side dependency mismatched with the client-side Dexie.js storage decision.
alternatives: alt-1-repository-pattern: Repository Pattern — cleaner boundaries but higher boilerplate, 5–8 files affected
alt-4-repository-with-in-memory-cache: Repository + Cache — most future-proof but same blast radius concern as alt-1
alt-2-active-record: Active Record via ORM — mismatched with client-side Dexie.js storage

adrs.title: Use In-Memory Memoization for Occurrence Caching
adrs.decision: Cache computed upcoming occurrences in a plain JavaScript Map keyed by templateId + date window within the useUpcomingOccurrences hook. Invalidate cache entries when a RecurringTemplate is created, updated, or deleted. No external caching infrastructure is introduced.
adrs.rationale: CashPulse is a client-side app with no backend API server — HTTP Cache-Control headers (alt-2) are inapplicable since there are no HTTP endpoints to cache. The occurrence computation is a pure CPU-bound function (O(n) on template count for weekly/monthly/yearly math) that completes in sub-millisecond time for typical user data (<50 templates). An in-memory Map provides instant cache hits on dashboard re-renders after initial computation, satisfying the 500 ms dashboard load constraint (NFR-001) with zero new dependencies. The cache resets on page refresh, which is acceptable since cold computation is already fast. Redis (alt-4) is over-engineered for a single-user desktop app. Precomputed persisted occurrences (alt-3) add schema migration complexity for negligible performance gain given the computation is already sub-millisecond.
alternatives: alt-2: HTTP Cache-Control — inapplicable; no backend API server exists
alt-3: Precomputed Persisted Occurrences — schema migration overhead for negligible gain
alt-4: Redis Cache — infrastructure overhead disproportionate to single-user desktop context

decisions.rationale: Dexie.js (IndexedDB) eliminates the localStorage 5 MB storage ceiling that will eventually bite a personal finance app accumulating expense records. It adds only ~22 KB gzipped, provides async access compatible with React hooks, and includes a versioned schema migration system for adding the RecurringTemplate entity. The async read overhead (~5–20 ms) is well within the 500 ms dashboard load budget. Client-side storage matches the desktop-scoped, single-user nature of CashPulse without introducing network latency or auth requirements.

Tailwind CSS provides zero-runtime styling with built-in motion-reduce: variant for prefers-reduced-motion compliance and sr-only/focus-visible utilities for accessibility — directly satisfying NFR-003 and NFR-005. CashPulse's design tokens (colors, spacing, typography, radii, shadows, animation durations) map cleanly to tailwind.config.js theme.extend. This choice aligns with shadcn/ui (gap-component-library decision), which is built on Tailwind. CSS Modules lack built-in a11y utilities; styled-components adds runtime overhead conflicting with the dashboard performance constraint.

shadcn/ui's copy-into-repo model provides zero runtime bundle overhead and full ownership of component markup. Radix UI primitives deliver WCAG 2.1 AA keyboard navigation, focus trapping, and focus-return out of the box — directly satisfying NFR-003 for the recurrence toggle (Switch), manage-recurring drawer (Sheet), and config panel (Collapsible). The Tailwind-based styling integrates naturally with the Tailwind CSS decision. Low blast radius (~6 files) is appropriate for this brownfield enhancement.

Composable query builder hooks are the minimal-blast-radius option (~3 hook files + ~2 component files), keeping the existing Add Expense form flow untouched (NFR-002). Occurrence computation is isolated in a pure-computation hook (useUpcomingOccurrences) with no I/O, keeping dashboard load fast. Data integrity (NFR-004) is enforced by nullifying the recurringTemplateId FK on linked expenses when a template is deleted. The repository pattern introduces disproportionate boilerplate for the current 3-entity scope.

CashPulse has no backend API server — HTTP Cache-Control headers are inapplicable. In-memory memoization via a JS Map is the only zero-dependency option that works in a purely client-side context. Occurrence computation is sub-millisecond for typical template counts (<50), so the cache primarily benefits dashboard re-renders (instant cache hits vs. recomputation). Redis and precomputed persistence are over-engineered for a single-user desktop app with fast pure-JS computation.

implementationPatterns.title: Dexie.js via composable React hooks for all persistence
implementationPatterns.rule: All data reads and writes go through typed React hooks (useExpenses, useCategories, useRecurringTemplates) that wrap Dexie.js table operations. Components never import the Dexie db instance directly. Each hook returns { data, isLoading, error } for reads and mutation functions for writes. Use Dexie's useLiveQuery for reactive UI updates.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-store (Dexie.js) and gap-data-access-pattern (composable query builder hooks). Centralizing Dexie access in hooks ensures consistent async handling, prevents scattered db imports, and enables Dexie LiveQuery reactivity across all data-dependent components.
forbids: Direct Dexie db imports in React components
Synchronous localStorage reads for entity data

implementationPatterns.title: Tailwind via CashPulse design tokens only
implementationPatterns.rule: All styling uses Tailwind utility classes referencing CashPulse design tokens defined in tailwind.config.js theme.extend. Use only token-mapped values for colors (blue-600, gray-900, emerald-500, etc.), spacing (multiples of 4px base unit), border-radius (card: rounded-2xl/16px, button: rounded-[10px], badge: rounded-full, input: rounded-lg/8px), shadows (shadow-card, shadow-elevated), and typography (font-inter with tabular-nums for amounts). Arbitrary Tailwind values (e.g., bg-[#abc123]) are permitted only when a PRD-specified token has no Tailwind equivalent.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-styling-approach (Tailwind CSS). The PRD defines a precise design system with specific hex values, spacing multiples, radii, and shadows. Mapping these to Tailwind theme tokens ensures visual consistency across all components — both existing and new recurring-transaction UI — and prevents style divergence between frontend siblings.
forbids: Inline style objects for layout or color
Raw hex values in className strings when a token exists

implementationPatterns.title: Zod schemas at data mutation boundaries
implementationPatterns.rule: Define Zod schemas for each data entity (ExpenseSchema, CategorySchema, RecurringTemplateSchema) and validate all data at mutation boundaries — before writing to Dexie.js. Schemas enforce required fields, enum constraints (frequency: weekly|monthly|yearly; status: active|paused; paymentMethod: cash|debit_card|credit_card|upi_other), amount range (0.01–99999.99), note max length (100 chars), and date validity. Validation errors are returned as structured objects with field-level messages.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-access-pattern (composable hooks enforce data integrity). Even without a backend API, client-side data integrity requires validation at the persistence boundary. Zod provides runtime type checking with TypeScript inference, catching invalid data before it reaches IndexedDB. This is especially important for RecurringTemplate where frequency and status enums must be strictly enforced.
forbids: Writing to Dexie without schema validation
Ad-hoc type assertions (as RecurringTemplate) without validation

implementationPatterns.title: Toast for transient errors, inline messages for form validation
implementationPatterns.rule: Use toast notifications (bottom-center, auto-dismiss after 2s, white background, card shadow, 12px radius) for transient operational feedback (e.g., 'Currency updated to EUR', '✓ Exported', 'Recurring template paused'). Use inline field-level error messages (caption style, red-500 text, positioned below the invalid input) for form validation errors on the Add Expense and Recurrence Configuration forms. Never use alert() or modal dialogs for validation errors.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from the PRD's existing toast pattern (section 10, currency change toast) and the assumption ledger entry for 'Toast messages for minor errors, inline messages for form errors'. This pattern ensures consistent error/feedback UX across existing and new recurring-transaction UI.
forbids: alert() or window.confirm() for validation
Modal dialogs for field-level errors

implementationPatterns.title: Keyboard navigation and focus management for all interactive elements
implementationPatterns.rule: All interactive elements must have minimum 36×36px click targets. The recurrence toggle (Switch) must respond to Space/Enter and announce state via aria-checked. The Manage Recurring drawer (Sheet) must trap focus while open and return focus to the trigger element on close. Focus rings use 2px blue-600 outline with 2px offset. Use Radix UI primitives (Switch, Dialog, Collapsible) which provide these behaviors natively. Respect prefers-reduced-motion via Tailwind's motion-reduce: variant — replace animations with instant renders.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-component-library (shadcn/ui with Radix primitives) and NFR-003/NFR-005. Radix primitives handle ARIA roles and focus trapping out of the box, but developers must ensure they are used correctly (not replaced with custom div-based implementations) and that motion-reduce: is applied to all animation classes.
forbids: Custom div-based toggles without ARIA attributes
Drawers/modals without focus trapping

implementationPatterns.title: Pure function for recurring occurrence computation with memoization
implementationPatterns.rule: Compute upcoming occurrences from RecurringTemplate data using a pure function (no side effects, no I/O). The function accepts a template and a date window, returns an array of upcoming dates. Results are memoized in a Map keyed by templateId + window hash. The cache is invalidated when any RecurringTemplate is created, updated, or deleted. The useUpcomingOccurrences hook wraps this function and exposes the memoized results to components.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-caching-strategy (in-memory memoization) and gap-data-access-pattern (composable hooks). Keeping occurrence computation pure and memoized ensures sub-millisecond dashboard re-renders (NFR-001) and makes the logic independently testable. The memoization cache is session-scoped (resets on page refresh), which is acceptable since cold computation is already fast for typical template counts.

implementationPatterns.title: Soft-unlink on recurring template deletion — never cascade to historical expenses
implementationPatterns.rule: When deleting a RecurringTemplate, nullify the recurringTemplateId field on all linked Expense records before removing the template. Never cascade-delete Expense records. This applies to both 'delete single occurrence' (nullify only that expense's FK) and 'delete entire series' (nullify all linked expenses, then delete the template). Implement this in the useRecurringTemplates().remove() hook as an atomic Dexie transaction.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from NFR-004 (data integrity constraint) and gap-data-access-pattern. Past expense records are the user's financial history and must never be lost due to template management actions. Using a Dexie transaction ensures atomicity — if the FK nullification fails, the template deletion is rolled back.

implementationPatterns.title: Structured console logging for client-side operations
implementationPatterns.rule: Log all data mutations (create/update/delete for expenses, categories, recurring templates) and errors using a lightweight structured logger that outputs JSON-formatted messages to console in development. Each log entry includes: timestamp, action (e.g., 'recurringTemplate.create'), entityId, and relevant metadata. In production builds, suppress debug-level logs. Use a thin wrapper (lib/logger.ts) so the logging implementation can be swapped later.
implementationPatterns.rationale: The baseline catalog specifies structured JSON logging (logging-structured-pino), but Pino is a Node.js library inappropriate for a client-side-only app. This pattern adapts the structured logging principle to the browser context while maintaining the same JSON shape for future server-side migration.

implementationPatterns.title: Dexie versioned schema for brownfield migrations
implementationPatterns.rule: Define the Dexie database schema using explicit version numbers. Version 1 covers the existing Expense and Category stores. Version 2 adds the RecurringTemplate store and the recurringTemplateId index on the Expense store. Each version increment includes an upgrade function that migrates existing data. Never modify a published version's store definition — always increment the version number.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-store (Dexie.js). Brownfield projects must handle existing user data gracefully. Dexie's versioned schema system ensures users with existing data are migrated automatically on app load without data loss.

implementationPatterns.title: Use only PRD-defined animation timing tokens
implementationPatterns.rule: All CSS transitions and animations must use one of the PRD-defined timing tokens: 150ms (micro-interactions), 250ms (layout transitions), 350ms (screen transitions), 400ms (number counters), 600ms (chart renders). Map these to Tailwind's transitionDuration theme extension (duration-micro, duration-layout, duration-screen, duration-counter, duration-chart). Apply motion-reduce: variant to disable animations under prefers-reduced-motion. New recurring-transaction animations (recurrence config slide-down: 200ms rounds to duration-layout/250ms; drawer slide-in: 350ms uses duration-screen) must use the nearest token.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-styling-approach (Tailwind) and NFR-005 (animation constraint). The PRD specifies exact timing values; using arbitrary durations would create visual inconsistency. Rounding the PRD's 200ms slide-down to the 250ms layout token maintains consistency with the existing animation system.
forbids: Arbitrary duration values not in the token set
Animations without motion-reduce: fallback

implementationPatterns.title: Single-source enum definitions shared across schema, UI, and validation
implementationPatterns.rule: Define all enum values (frequency: weekly|monthly|yearly, status: active|paused, paymentMethod: cash|debit_card|credit_card|upi_other) as TypeScript const arrays in a shared types module. Derive Zod enum schemas, TypeScript union types, and UI chip/badge labels from the same source array. Never duplicate enum values in component files.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-access-pattern and validation-zod-at-boundary. Enum drift between the Dexie schema, Zod validation, and UI components is a common source of bugs in brownfield projects. A single source of truth eliminates this class of errors.

stackConfig.frontend: React with TypeScript, shadcn/ui (Radix UI primitives)
stackConfig.backend: None (client-side only — all logic runs in the browser)
stackConfig.styling: Tailwind CSS with CashPulse design tokens mapped to theme.extend
stackConfig.componentLibrary: shadcn/ui (copy-into-repo, built on Radix UI primitives)

Add the structured logger wrapper.
