alternatives.name: In-Memory Store with localStorage Persistence
alternatives.description: Extend the existing in-memory state management (e.g., React context or Zustand store) to persist all three entities (Expense, Category, RecurringTemplate) to localStorage via JSON serialization. RecurringTemplate records are stored as a flat JSON array alongside existing Expense/Category data. Upcoming occurrences are computed on-the-fly at dashboard load time using a pure JS date-math utility — no DB query needed.
tradeoffs: Minimal blast radius: only the existing store module and one new RecurringTemplate slice need changes (~3–5 files affected: store/expenses.ts, store/categories.ts, new store/recurringTemplates.ts, utils/recurrenceEngine.ts, and the persistence middleware).
Zero new runtime dependencies — localStorage is a browser native API.
Occurrence computation is synchronous and sub-millisecond for typical datasets (<500 expenses), satisfying the dashboard performance constraint.
Storage cap: localStorage is limited to ~5 MB per origin; large expense histories could eventually hit this ceiling, but is unlikely for a personal finance app in v1.
No cross-device or cross-browser sync — data is siloed to the browser profile.
Data loss risk on cache/storage clear; no backup or export mechanism (out of scope but worth noting).
Querying (e.g., filtering expenses by template) requires full in-memory scan — acceptable at this scale but does not scale beyond ~10k records.

alternatives.name: SQLite via Origin Private File System (OPFS) with sql.js or wa-sqlite
alternatives.description: Embed a WebAssembly SQLite engine (sql.js or wa-sqlite) that persists a .sqlite file to the browser's Origin Private File System. All three entities are modelled as proper relational tables with foreign keys (e.g., expenses.recurring_template_id → recurring_templates.id). Upcoming occurrences are computed in a Web Worker to avoid blocking the main thread, satisfying the dashboard performance constraint.
tradeoffs: Medium blast radius: requires adding a Web Worker file, a WASM loader, a DB initialization module, and updating all data-access layers (~8–12 files: db/init.ts, db/migrations/001_add_recurring.sql, workers/recurrenceWorker.ts, repositories/expenseRepo.ts, repositories/categoryRepo.ts, repositories/recurringTemplateRepo.ts, plus updates to all store/context files that currently hold data).
Adds 2 new dependencies: sql.js (~1.5 MB WASM) or wa-sqlite (~400 KB WASM) — increases initial bundle/load size noticeably; requires async WASM initialization before the app is interactive.
Enables proper relational integrity: deleting a RecurringTemplate can be constrained to SET NULL on future expense records, directly satisfying the data integrity constraint.
OPFS is supported in all modern browsers (Chrome 86+, Firefox 111+, Safari 15.2+) but requires careful feature-detection fallback.
Web Worker message-passing adds architectural complexity (async IPC pattern) but keeps the main thread free — dashboard load remains snappy.
Migration path to a server-side DB (e.g., PostgreSQL) is cleaner because the repository pattern is already in place.
No cross-device sync; still browser-local storage.

alternatives.name: Supabase (PostgreSQL) Backend
alternatives.description: Introduce a Supabase project as the persistence layer. Expense, Category, and RecurringTemplate are modelled as PostgreSQL tables with row-level security (RLS) policies. A Supabase Edge Function or a PostgreSQL generated column computes the next_occurrence date for each template. The dashboard fetches upcoming recurrences via a single Supabase RPC call or a view, keeping dashboard load fast.
tradeoffs: High blast radius: requires a Supabase project setup, schema migrations, RLS policy definitions, Supabase client initialization, and replacement of all local data-access calls with async Supabase queries (~15–20 files affected across store, repository, and component layers, plus new supabase/migrations/ directory).
Adds 1 new dependency: @supabase/supabase-js (~50 KB gzipped) — modest bundle impact.
Requires network connectivity — offline use is broken unless a local cache layer (e.g., React Query with stale-while-revalidate) is added, which is an additional ~1 dependency and pattern.
Enables cross-device sync, multi-user potential, and durable cloud backup — significant future-proofing beyond the current desktop-scoped constraint.

Dashboard performance depends on network latency; a materialized view or pre-computed next_occurrence column on RecurringTemplate mitigates this, but cold-start latency on free-tier Supabase (~200–500 ms) may be perceptible.
Introduces auth complexity: Supabase Auth or anonymous sessions must be configured to scope RLS policies, even for a single-user app.
Data integrity constraint (delete template → preserve past expenses) is trivially enforced via ON DELETE SET NULL foreign key.
Overkill for a desktop-scoped, single-user personal finance app in this version, but the right choice if multi-device or SaaS is on the roadmap.

alternatives.name: IndexedDB with Dexie.js
alternatives.description: Replace or augment the current persistence layer with IndexedDB accessed through the Dexie.js wrapper. Define three object stores (expenses, categories, recurringTemplates) with compound indexes (e.g., [templateId+date] on expenses). Upcoming occurrences are computed in-process using a lightweight recurrence engine; Dexie's liveQuery enables reactive dashboard updates without polling.
tradeoffs: Low-to-medium blast radius: Dexie wraps IndexedDB with a familiar promise/async API, minimising rewrite surface. Existing store/context files are updated to call Dexie instead of localStorage (~6–9 files: db/cashpulse.db.ts defining the Dexie schema, plus updates to store/expenses.ts, store/categories.ts, new store/recurringTemplates.ts, utils/recurrenceEngine.ts, and any component hooks that read data).
Adds 1 new dependency: dexie (~25 KB gzipped) — negligible bundle impact.
IndexedDB storage quota is much larger than localStorage (typically 60%+ of available disk), eliminating the 5 MB ceiling concern.
Dexie's liveQuery makes the dashboard card reactively update when template data changes — no manual cache invalidation needed.

Compound indexes allow efficient queries like 'all expenses for templateId X after date Y', directly supporting the data integrity constraint (past expenses preserved on template delete via a soft-delete or SET NULL pattern).
Occurrence computation remains synchronous JS — no WASM overhead, no Web Worker required for typical dataset sizes.
IndexedDB is async by nature; all reads/writes return Promises, requiring async/await patterns throughout data-access code (a refactor cost if current code is synchronous localStorage).
Still browser-local; no cross-device sync.

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency minimal change to full cloud backend, covering the realistic decision space for a brownfield desktop-scoped personal finance app. Two alternatives (alt-1 localStorage, alt-4 Dexie/IndexedDB) are "minimal change" options that keep the stack local and dependency-light — appropriate given the desktop-scoped, single-user constraints and the soft performance requirement. Alt-2 (OPFS/SQLite WASM) was included because it offers relational integrity and a migration path without a server, at the cost of WASM complexity. Alt-3 (Supabase) was included as the "future-proof" option for completeness, though it is likely over-engineered for this version. Excluded alternatives: (1) PouchDB/CouchDB — adds sync complexity not needed here; (2) plain file-system APIs without WASM SQLite — too low-level and non-standard; (3) server-side Node + PostgreSQL without Supabase — requires a backend server process, which is out of scope for what appears to be a client-only app.
structured.recommendation: alt-4 — Dexie.js over IndexedDB. It is the best balance of minimal blast radius (low, ~6–9 files), a single small dependency (25 KB), significantly larger storage quota than localStorage, built-in reactive queries (liveQuery) that keep the dashboard card fast and live, and compound indexes that make the data integrity constraint (preserve past expenses on template delete) straightforward to implement. It avoids the WASM initialization overhead of alt-2 and the network/auth complexity of alt-3, while being a meaningful upgrade over raw localStorage (alt-1) in terms of query capability and storage headroom. The async refactor cost is the main tradeoff, but it is a one-time, well-scoped effort.

alternatives.name: CSS Modules (Minimal Change — Extend Existing Pattern)
alternatives.description: Assume CashPulse already uses CSS Modules (a common brownfield React default). All new UI elements — the recurrence toggle, config slide-down panel, Manage Recurring drawer, and dashboard recurring card — are styled with co-located `.module.css` files. Animation tokens (timing, easing) are shared via CSS custom properties already defined in a global `:root` stylesheet. The `prefers-reduced-motion` media query is handled directly in each module file. No new dependencies are introduced.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies — uses what the build system already supports (Webpack/Vite CSS Modules)
Scoped class names prevent style leakage into existing Expense, Category, or Dashboard components
Verbose for highly dynamic styles (e.g., conditional animation states require multiple class toggles in JS)
Sharing design tokens across modules requires a global CSS variables file — must ensure it already exists or create one (1 new file)
Affected files: ~8 new `.module.css` files + 1 shared token file if absent; 0 existing files modified for styling
Animation: `prefers-reduced-motion` must be duplicated in each module unless a shared mixin/utility class is established
No runtime style computation — best raw performance for the Dashboard recurring card render path

alternatives.name: Tailwind CSS (Utility-First, Greenfield-Friendly)
alternatives.description: Introduce Tailwind CSS as the styling layer for all new recurring-transaction UI: the recurrence toggle, slide-down config section, Manage Recurring drawer, and dashboard card. Tailwind's `motion-safe` / `motion-reduce` variants handle the `prefers-reduced-motion` constraint declaratively. Animation timing tokens are mapped to `tailwind.config.js` `extend.transitionDuration` and `extend.transitionTimingFunction`. If Tailwind is not already in the project, this requires installation and PostCSS configuration.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new dependency (`tailwindcss`) + 2 peer dependencies (`postcss`, `autoprefixer`) if not already present — requires PostCSS pipeline setup

If Tailwind is already in the stack, blast radius is near-zero for tooling; if not, requires modifying `vite.config.js`/`webpack.config.js` and adding `tailwind.config.js` + `postcss.config.js` (4 config files touched)
Utility classes inline in JSX improve co-location but can make JSX verbose for complex components like the drawer (focus-trap styles, ARIA-driven visibility)
`motion-safe:` and `motion-reduce:` variants provide first-class `prefers-reduced-motion` support with no extra CSS authoring
Purging/tree-shaking requires correct `content` glob in `tailwind.config.js` — misconfiguration causes bloated CSS in production
Risk of style inconsistency if existing components use a different methodology (e.g., CSS Modules + Tailwind mixed) — requires a team convention decision
Affected files: ~6 JSX component files (new), 4 config files (if greenfield install), 0 existing component files

alternatives.name: styled-components (CSS-in-JS, Runtime Styling)
alternatives.description: Use styled-components to author all new recurring-transaction UI with co-located, component-scoped styles. Dynamic styles (e.g., slide-down height animation driven by a `isOpen` prop, toggle active state) are expressed cleanly as prop-interpolated template literals. A shared `theme` object provides animation timing tokens. The `createGlobalStyle` helper can inject a `prefers-reduced-motion` override globally.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new runtime dependency (`styled-components` ~12 kB gzipped) — increases JS bundle size, which has a minor but measurable impact on Dashboard initial load performance

Runtime CSS injection (style tags generated at render time) introduces a small per-render cost — acceptable for static UI but worth profiling on the Dashboard recurring card which may render multiple occurrence rows
Excellent ergonomics for dynamic prop-driven styles (e.g., `isOpen`, `isReduced` for motion) — reduces JS class-toggling logic in component files
If the existing codebase uses CSS Modules or plain CSS, mixing styled-components creates two styling paradigms — increases cognitive overhead for future contributors
SSR requires `ServerStyleSheet` setup — relevant only if CashPulse moves to SSR; currently a non-issue for a client-side dashboard app
Affected files: ~6 new styled component files or co-located styled definitions, 1 theme file (new or extended), `package.json` + lock file
Focus-trap and ARIA state for the Manage Recurring drawer must still be handled in JS (e.g., `focus-trap-react`) — styled-components does not help with behavior, only appearance

alternatives.name: Inline Styles + CSS Custom Properties (Zero-Dependency Escape Hatch)
alternatives.description: For the smallest possible footprint, new components use React inline styles for dynamic values (e.g., drawer open/close transform, toggle checked state color) combined with CSS custom properties defined in a single new global stylesheet for static design tokens (colors, spacing, timing). Animation is handled via a single shared `.recurring-animations.css` file with `@keyframes` and `prefers-reduced-motion` overrides. No new build tooling or dependencies required.
tradeoffs: Zero new dependencies and zero build configuration changes — safest option for a brownfield codebase with unknown tooling constraints

Inline styles cannot use pseudo-classes (`:hover`, `:focus-visible`) or media queries directly — requires supplemental CSS class for focus ring on the toggle (accessibility constraint), adding 1 small CSS file
No style encapsulation — the shared `recurring-animations.css` file uses global class names, risking collision with existing styles (mitigated by a `rct-` namespace prefix convention)
Dynamic animation via inline `style` prop (e.g., `transform: translateY(${isOpen ? 0 : -100}%)`) works but bypasses CSS transitions unless paired with a CSS class — requires careful implementation
Hardest to maintain at scale: design token changes require updating both the CSS variables file and any hardcoded fallbacks in inline styles
Affected files: 1 new global CSS file (`recurring-animations.css`), ~6 JSX component files (new), 1 entry CSS file to import the new global (existing file, 1-line change)
Best suited if the team wants to defer the styling methodology decision and ship the feature quickly

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-dependency to full CSS-in-JS, covering the realistic options for a brownfield React app. Sass/LESS preprocessors were excluded because they require build pipeline changes similar to Tailwind but offer fewer modern benefits (CSS custom properties now cover most token-sharing use cases natively). Vanilla Extract and Linaria were excluded as they are compile-time CSS-in-JS solutions that, while performant, introduce significant new tooling complexity disproportionate to the scope of this enhancement (6 screens, 3 entities). Stitches was excluded as it is effectively unmaintained. The four selected alternatives directly map to the most common brownfield React styling patterns and each can be evaluated against the project's soft constraints: Dashboard load performance (favors CSS Modules or inline styles over runtime CSS-in-JS), animation system integration (all four can honor timing tokens and prefers-reduced-motion), and accessibility (all four are styling-layer agnostic — focus trap and ARIA behavior are handled in JS regardless of choice).
structured.recommendation: alt-css-modules — CSS Modules is the recommended choice for this brownfield enhancement. It almost certainly matches the existing codebase's styling approach (the most common default in React projects scaffolded with CRA or Vite), introduces zero new dependencies, imposes no runtime cost on the Dashboard recurring card render path (directly addressing the performance soft constraint), and provides full scoping to prevent regressions in existing Expense and Category UI. Animation timing tokens shared via CSS custom properties on `:root` satisfy the animation system constraint cleanly. The `prefers-reduced-motion` media query is a single block per module file. If the team discovers the existing codebase already uses Tailwind, alt-tailwind becomes the obvious choice with near-zero additional blast radius.

alternatives.name: shadcn/ui (copy-into-project model)
alternatives.description: Adopt shadcn/ui as the component foundation. Components are copied directly into the codebase (not installed as a black-box npm package), built on Radix UI primitives and styled with Tailwind CSS utility classes. New recurring-transaction UI (toggle switch, drawer, config slide-down, dashboard card) is built from shadcn primitives such as Switch, Sheet, Collapsible, and Card. Existing components are migrated or co-located incrementally.
tradeoffs: Requires Tailwind CSS to already be configured or adds it as a new dependency — if not present, this is a significant setup cost affecting build config, postcss, and all existing stylesheets
Components live in /components/ui/ as owned source files — full control but means bug fixes from upstream must be manually pulled in (no automatic updates)

Radix UI primitives handle keyboard accessibility and focus trapping out of the box, directly satisfying the Switch (Space/Enter) and Drawer (focus trap + return focus) accessibility constraints with zero custom code
Sheet (drawer) and Collapsible (recurrence config slide-down) ship with accessible ARIA roles and prefers-reduced-motion support via Radix, satisfying the animation constraint with minimal effort
Blast radius: ~8–12 files (new /components/ui/* primitives, Add Expense form, Dashboard card, Manage Recurring drawer, Settings dialog, global CSS/tailwind config)
If the existing app already uses a different styling system (e.g., CSS Modules, Emotion), mixing Tailwind introduces a dual-styling-system problem across all touched files
Zero runtime bundle cost for unused components — only copied primitives are shipped

alternatives.name: Mantine UI (full npm package)
alternatives.description: Install Mantine (@mantine/core, @mantine/hooks) as the component library. Use Mantine's Switch, Drawer, Collapse, and Card components for all new recurring-transaction UI. Mantine ships its own CSS-in-JS (or CSS Modules in v7+) styling system and provides a ThemeProvider for design tokens. Existing components can be migrated opportunistically or left as-is.
tradeoffs: Adds 2 new production dependencies (@mantine/core, @mantine/hooks) plus a peer dependency on @mantine/styles or PostCSS plugin depending on version — increases bundle size by ~40–80 KB gzipped
Mantine v7+ uses CSS Modules natively, which may conflict with an existing Emotion/styled-components setup and requires PostCSS configuration changes
Drawer component includes built-in focus trapping and return-focus behavior; Switch is keyboard accessible by default — accessibility constraints met without custom implementation
Collapse component supports CSS transitions and respects prefers-reduced-motion when configured with the Mantine theme's respectMotionPreference flag
ThemeProvider wraps the entire app — requires a root-level change to App.tsx or _app.tsx, touching the application shell
Blast radius: ~10–15 files (App root, Add Expense form, Dashboard card, Manage Recurring drawer, Settings dialog, theme config file, PostCSS config)
Mantine's opinionated default styles may visually conflict with existing custom components unless a CSS reset strategy is agreed upon
Excellent TypeScript support and comprehensive documentation reduce implementation risk for new developers

alternatives.name: Headless UI + existing styles (minimal-change)
alternatives.description: Install only @headlessui/react (a zero-style, fully accessible headless library from the Tailwind Labs team) to supply the behavioral primitives needed: Switch (toggle), Dialog (drawer/focus trap), Disclosure (recurrence config slide-down). All visual styling is applied using whatever CSS system already exists in the project (CSS Modules, plain CSS, Emotion, etc.). No design system is introduced. This is the minimal-blast-radius option.
tradeoffs: Adds exactly 1 new production dependency (@headlessui/react) — minimal bundle impact (~13 KB gzipped)
Zero styling opinions — all visual design must be hand-authored using the existing CSS system, which means more CSS work per component but no style conflicts
Headless UI Switch and Dialog are fully ARIA-compliant and keyboard accessible out of the box; Dialog handles focus trapping and return-focus natively — accessibility constraints met

Animation is handled via Headless UI's Transition component, which can be wired to existing timing tokens via CSS classes; prefers-reduced-motion must be handled manually in CSS (e.g., @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { transition: none })
Blast radius: ~5–7 files (Add Expense form, Dashboard card, Manage Recurring drawer, one shared CSS file for new component styles) — lowest of all alternatives
No dashboard card or form layout components are provided — developers must build these from scratch using existing patterns, increasing implementation time vs. a full component library
Best choice if the existing codebase has a strong, consistent CSS system and the team wants to avoid introducing a new design language

alternatives.name: Material UI (MUI v5/v6)
alternatives.description: Adopt MUI (@mui/material) as the component library. Use MUI's Switch, Drawer, Collapse, and Card components for all new recurring-transaction UI. MUI uses Emotion for styling by default and ships a comprehensive theming system. This is the most feature-complete option but carries the highest integration cost in a brownfield project.
tradeoffs: Adds 3–4 new production dependencies (@mui/material, @mui/system, @emotion/react, @emotion/styled) — largest bundle impact of all alternatives (~90–130 KB gzipped for tree-shaken usage)
If the existing project already uses Emotion, this is additive; if it uses a different CSS system, dual-styling-system complexity is introduced across all files
MUI's Drawer uses a Modal with focus trap (FocusTrap component) and returns focus on close — accessibility constraints met; Switch is keyboard accessible by default

MUI's Collapse and Drawer support sx prop and theme transitions; prefers-reduced-motion can be respected via MUI's theme.transitions.create with a custom easing, but requires explicit theme configuration

MUI's opinionated Material Design aesthetic is the hardest to override to match a custom brand — requires significant theme customization to avoid a 'Material Design look' that clashes with existing UI

Blast radius: ~12–18 files (App root ThemeProvider, Add Expense form, Dashboard card, Manage Recurring drawer, Settings dialog, theme file, potentially existing components that need style reconciliation)
Best justified only if the existing project already uses MUI — introducing it fresh into a brownfield app for a single feature enhancement is disproportionate to the scope

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from minimal-change to full design-system adoption, covering the realistic decision space for a brownfield React app. Alternatives were selected because each maps directly to the specific new UI primitives required by this feature (toggle switch, drawer, collapsible config panel, dashboard card) and each has a meaningfully different integration cost profile. Pure CSS / no-library was excluded because the accessibility constraints (focus trapping, ARIA roles, keyboard navigation) are non-trivial to implement correctly from scratch and would introduce maintenance risk. Ant Design was excluded because it is primarily Asia-Pacific market-oriented with a distinct aesthetic that rarely fits Western SaaS products and carries a similar bundle cost to MUI without the ecosystem breadth. Chakra UI was excluded because it is functionally similar to Mantine but with a smaller community and less active v3 maintenance trajectory, making Mantine the strictly better choice in that tier.
structured.recommendation: alt-headlessui-minimal for most brownfield scenarios, with alt-shadcn-ui as the preferred upgrade path if Tailwind CSS is already present. Headless UI minimizes blast radius (5–7 files, 1 dependency), satisfies all accessibility and animation constraints natively, and avoids introducing a competing design language into an existing codebase. If the project already uses Tailwind, shadcn/ui is the stronger choice because it provides ready-made Card, Sheet, and Collapsible components that directly map to the dashboard card, Manage Recurring drawer, and recurrence config slide-down — reducing implementation time significantly while keeping the copy-into-project model's full customizability.

alternatives.name: Repository Pattern with Typed Repositories per Entity
alternatives.description: Introduce a dedicated repository class for each data entity (ExpenseRepository, CategoryRepository, RecurringTemplateRepository). Each repository encapsulates all query logic, CRUD operations, and relationship traversal for its entity. The RecurringTemplateRepository owns the logic for computing upcoming occurrences (e.g., nextOccurrences(templateId, n)), keeping dashboard-loading concerns isolated. Repositories are injected into service/hook layers and never called directly from UI components.
tradeoffs: Adds 3 new repository files (ExpenseRepository, CategoryRepository, RecurringTemplateRepository) and 3 corresponding interface/type files — 6 new files total
Requires refactoring existing direct data-access calls in UI hooks/components to route through repositories — estimated 4–8 existing files touched
Occurrence computation logic lives in RecurringTemplateRepository.nextOccurrences(), making it easily unit-testable in isolation without UI setup
Clear separation means dashboard card can call a single repository method with memoization/caching, satisfying the perceptible-delay constraint
Deleting a RecurringTemplate is a single repository method that can enforce the data-integrity rule (soft-delete or nullify FK on future occurrences only) in one place
Highest upfront boilerplate of all alternatives, but lowest long-term coupling
references: Fowler, M. — Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture (Repository pattern, p. 322)

alternatives.name: Centralized Query Builder / Data-Access Layer (DAL)
alternatives.description: Introduce a single queryBuilder module (e.g., db/queries.ts) that exports named, composable query functions (getExpensesByCategory, getRecurringTemplates, getUpcomingOccurrences, etc.). No class hierarchy — just pure functions that accept filter/sort parameters and return typed results. The RecurringTemplate–Expense relationship is handled via explicit join-style functions rather than ORM associations. This is the lightest structured approach that still centralizes data access.
tradeoffs: Adds 1 new file (db/queries.ts or similar) plus 1 types file — minimal footprint of 2 new files
Existing ad-hoc queries in hooks/components are migrated to named exports in the DAL — estimated 4–8 existing files touched (same as repository pattern)
No class instantiation or DI overhead; functions are tree-shakeable and easy to mock in tests
Occurrence computation (getUpcomingOccurrences) is a pure function — trivially memoizable with useMemo or React Query's staleTime, directly addressing the dashboard performance constraint
Less enforced structure than repositories: as the codebase grows, queries.ts can become a 'god file' without discipline
Relationship integrity rules (e.g., delete template → preserve past expenses) must be documented as conventions rather than enforced by class boundaries
Easiest to adopt incrementally — new RecurringTemplate queries are additive, existing Expense/Category queries are untouched until refactored
references: Kysely — Type-safe SQL query builder for TypeScript: https://kysely.dev/
Drizzle ORM query builder pattern: https://orm.drizzle.team/docs/rqb

alternatives.name: Active Record Pattern via ORM Model Classes
alternatives.description: Leverage an ORM (e.g., Prisma, TypeORM, or Drizzle with relational queries) where each entity (Expense, Category, RecurringTemplate) is a model with built-in finders and associations. Relationships (Expense belongsTo RecurringTemplate, RecurringTemplate hasMany Expenses) are declared on the model. Occurrence computation is a static method on RecurringTemplate. UI hooks call model methods directly (RecurringTemplate.findWithUpcoming()).
tradeoffs: If an ORM is already in the stack: 0 new dependencies, 3 model definitions updated/created — blast radius is low to medium

If no ORM is currently used: adds 1 major dependency (e.g., Prisma adds prisma + @prisma/client, schema.prisma, migration tooling) — 1 new dependency, schema migration required, significant brownfield integration cost
Active Record tightly couples data-access logic to model classes, making unit testing harder without a real or in-memory database

ORM-generated queries for relationship traversal (template → upcoming expenses) may be less optimized than hand-written queries, risking the dashboard performance constraint if occurrence sets are large
Cascade delete rules (preserve past expenses on template deletion) must be configured at the ORM/DB level — risk of misconfiguration violating the data-integrity constraint
Fastest to write for simple CRUD; relationship queries (e.g., expenses linked to a template) are ergonomic with ORM associations
references: Prisma ORM — Data modeling and relations: https://www.prisma.io/docs/concepts/components/prisma-schema/relations
Fowler, M. — Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture (Active Record, p. 160)

alternatives.name: Minimal Change — Inline Data Access in Custom Hooks (Status Quo Extension)
alternatives.description: Extend the existing pattern (whatever it is — likely direct fetch/localStorage/IndexedDB calls inside custom React hooks) by adding new hooks for RecurringTemplate operations (useRecurringTemplates, useUpcomingOccurrences) without introducing any new architectural layer. Existing Expense and Category hooks are untouched. Occurrence computation is a utility function co-located with the new hook. This is the lowest-blast-radius option and the fastest to ship.
tradeoffs: Zero new architectural dependencies or patterns — 0 new libraries, 0 existing files modified for Expense/Category
Adds ~2–3 new hook files (useRecurringTemplates.ts, useUpcomingOccurrences.ts) and 1 utility file (computeOccurrences.ts) — minimal footprint
Occurrence computation utility is a pure function (easy to unit test) but lives outside any shared data layer, so it cannot be reused if a future screen needs the same data
Data-integrity rule (preserve past expenses on template delete) must be implemented inside the delete handler in the hook — no structural enforcement, relies on developer discipline
Highest risk of divergence: Expense hooks and RecurringTemplate hooks may evolve inconsistently, increasing future refactor cost when auto-generation (deferred scope) is added
Dashboard performance constraint is met by memoizing the occurrence computation utility with useMemo — straightforward with no new infrastructure
Best choice if the team wants to validate the RecurringTemplate feature before committing to a data-access architecture refactor
references: Kent C. Dodds — Application State Management with React: https://kentcdodds.com/blog/application-state-management-with-react
You Might Not Need an ORM: https://www.prisma.io/dataguide/types/relational/comparing-sql-query-builders-and-orms

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero architectural change (inline hooks) to full ORM adoption (Active Record), covering the realistic decision space for a brownfield desktop app adding a single new entity (RecurringTemplate) with a relationship to an existing entity (Expense). Excluded alternatives: (1) Event Sourcing / CQRS — massively over-engineered for a 3-entity personal finance app with no distributed system requirements. (2) GraphQL data layer — introduces a server-side concern that is out of scope for what appears to be a client-side/local-storage app. (3) Full microservice data isolation — irrelevant at this scale. The four selected alternatives are ordered by blast radius (low → high) and each is implementable without changing the existing Expense or Category data structures, which is critical for a brownfield project. The soft constraints most relevant to this decision are: dashboard performance (favors pure/memoizable occurrence computation), data integrity on template deletion (favors centralized enforcement), and the scope exclusion of auto-generation (means the data layer only needs to read/compute occurrences, not write them — keeping complexity low).
structured.recommendation: alt-query-builder — The centralized query builder (DAL) hits the sweet spot for this project. It adds minimal files (1–2), keeps occurrence computation as a pure memoizable function (satisfying the dashboard performance constraint), enforces a single place for the template-deletion integrity rule, and is fully additive to the brownfield codebase without touching existing Expense/Category hooks. It avoids the class-hierarchy overhead of repositories while providing more structure than inline hooks — making it easier to extend when auto-generation is added in a future version. If the team is already using an ORM, alt-repository-pattern becomes equally attractive. If the team wants to ship the fastest possible spike to validate the RecurringTemplate UX, alt-minimal-inline-hooks is the right starting point with a planned migration to the query builder once the feature is proven.

alternatives.name: In-Memory Memoization (Minimal Change)
alternatives.description: Cache computed upcoming occurrences for each RecurringTemplate in a plain JavaScript Map or object keyed by template ID + computation date. On Dashboard load, the occurrence-generation function checks the cache first; if the entry is fresh (e.g., TTL ≤ 5 minutes), it returns the cached result without recomputing. Cache lives in the API route handler module scope (Node.js process memory) and is invalidated on template create/update/delete mutations. No new infrastructure is required.
tradeoffs: Zero new runtime dependencies — uses native JS Map
Cache is per-process: in multi-instance deployments (e.g., serverless cold starts) each instance warms its own cache independently, so the first request per instance still pays full compute cost
Cache is lost on server restart or cold start — acceptable given the low compute cost of occurrence generation for weekly/monthly/yearly schedules
Invalidation is straightforward: mutation handlers call cache.delete(templateId) — affects ~3 files (mutation handler, cache module, dashboard API route)
No persistence: cache cannot be shared across users or sessions, but for a per-user desktop app this is a non-issue
TTL management must be hand-rolled (~20 lines of utility code)
Blast radius: ~3–4 files (new cache.ts utility, dashboard query handler, template mutation handlers)

alternatives.name: Next.js / Framework Data Cache with Revalidation Tags
alternatives.description: Leverage the built-in fetch/data cache provided by the existing framework (e.g., Next.js App Router Data Cache or React Query on the client). Tag dashboard occurrence queries with a cache tag such as 'recurring-occurrences'. On any RecurringTemplate mutation (create, update, delete), call revalidateTag('recurring-occurrences'). The framework handles TTL, storage, and invalidation automatically. This is the idiomatic approach if the project already uses Next.js 13+ App Router or a similar framework with first-class cache primitives.
tradeoffs: Zero new infrastructure — fully leverages existing framework cache layer
Declarative invalidation via tags is clean and co-located with mutations — easy to reason about
Requires the project to already be on Next.js 13+ App Router (or equivalent); if on Pages Router or a different framework, this approach is not available without upgrading

Cache is scoped to the server-side data cache, not the client — client-side state management (e.g., React Query) would need a separate invalidation signal (e.g., query key invalidation on mutation success)
Adds coupling between mutation handlers and cache tag strings — a typo in a tag name silently breaks invalidation; affects ~5–6 files (each mutation handler + dashboard fetch function)
Framework cache behavior differs between dev and production — requires careful testing
Blast radius: ~5–6 files (dashboard server component/fetch, 3 template mutation handlers, shared cache-tags constant file)

alternatives.name: Client-Side Query Cache with React Query (TanStack Query)
alternatives.description: Move occurrence computation to a client-side query managed by TanStack Query (React Query). The dashboard fetches pre-computed upcoming occurrences from a lightweight API endpoint (GET /api/recurring-occurrences). React Query caches the response in memory on the client with a configurable staleTime (e.g., 5 minutes). Template mutations call queryClient.invalidateQueries(['recurring-occurrences']) to trigger a background refetch. This approach is ideal if the app already uses React Query for other data fetching, making it a natural extension.
tradeoffs: If React Query is already in the stack, blast radius is minimal — adds 1 new query key and 3 invalidation call-sites (~4–5 files)
If React Query is NOT already in the stack, adds 1 new dependency (tanstack/react-query ~13 kB gzipped) and requires a QueryClientProvider wrapper — affects app root and potentially many files
Client-side cache means the first page load always fetches from the server, but subsequent navigations within the session are instant
staleTime and gcTime are easily tunable without code changes to business logic
Invalidation on mutation is explicit and type-safe if query keys are centralized in a constants file
Occurrence computation still happens server-side (in the API route), keeping the client bundle lean
Background refetch on window focus may cause unexpected network requests — should be configured (refetchOnWindowFocus: false) for this use case
Blast radius: ~4–5 files if React Query already present; ~8–10 files if introducing it fresh (root provider, all existing fetches to migrate or coexist)

alternatives.name: Redis Distributed Cache
alternatives.description: Introduce a Redis instance (e.g., Upstash Redis for serverless, or a managed Redis on Railway/Render) as a shared cache layer. Computed upcoming occurrences are serialized to JSON and stored in Redis with a key pattern like occurrences:{userId}:{templateId} and a TTL of 10–15 minutes. Template mutations issue DEL or UNLINK commands to invalidate affected keys. This approach is appropriate if CashPulse is multi-tenant, multi-instance, or expects high concurrency.
tradeoffs: Adds 1–2 new dependencies (e.g., ioredis or @upstash/redis) and requires provisioning and managing a Redis instance — operational overhead
Introduces network latency on cache reads (~1–5 ms for Upstash, ~0.1–0.5 ms for co-located Redis) — negligible but non-zero
Solves the multi-instance cache coherence problem that in-memory caching cannot — all server instances share the same cache
Overkill for a single-user desktop-scoped app (per scope constraints) — the complexity is not justified by the concurrency requirements
Requires secrets management for Redis connection strings in all environments (dev, staging, prod)
Cache serialization/deserialization of Date objects in occurrence lists requires careful handling (JSON does not preserve Date types)
Blast radius: ~8–10 files (new redis client module, dashboard query handler, 3 mutation handlers, environment config, deployment config, potentially CI pipeline)

structured.rationale: These four alternatives span the full spectrum from zero-infrastructure to full distributed caching, covering the realistic decision space for a brownfield desktop-scoped personal finance app. Redis (alt-4) was included for completeness but is explicitly flagged as overkill given the scope constraints (desktop-only, single-user, no auto-generation of expenses). A CDN/HTTP cache layer (e.g., Cache-Control headers + CDN) was excluded because the occurrence data is user-specific and personalized, making shared HTTP caching ineffective and potentially a data-privacy risk. A service worker cache was excluded because the app is desktop-scoped and the existing architecture shows no PWA/offline requirements. The recommended path is alt-2 (framework data cache) if the project is on Next.js App Router, or alt-1 (in-memory memoization) if framework cache primitives are unavailable — both keep blast radius low and require no new infrastructure, directly satisfying the performance soft constraint without disrupting the existing quick-entry flow.
structured.recommendation: alt-2 — Framework Data Cache with Revalidation Tags is the preferred choice if the stack supports it (Next.js 13+ App Router). It is idiomatic, requires zero new dependencies or infrastructure, and provides automatic invalidation via tags co-located with mutations. The declarative model keeps the caching concern out of business logic. If the framework cache is unavailable, fall back to alt-1 (in-memory memoization), which has the smallest blast radius of any option and is fully sufficient for a single-user, desktop-scoped app where occurrence generation for weekly/monthly/yearly schedules is computationally trivial.

adrs.title: Use IndexedDB via Dexie.js for Client-Side Persistence
adrs.decision: Adopt Dexie.js as the persistence layer for all three entities (Expense, Category, RecurringTemplate). Define three object stores with compound indexes (e.g., [recurringTemplateId+date] on expenses). Use Dexie's liveQuery for reactive dashboard updates.
adrs.rationale: CashPulse is a desktop-scoped, single-user, client-side app. Dexie.js over IndexedDB provides significantly larger storage quota than localStorage (~60%+ of disk vs 5 MB), built-in reactive queries (liveQuery) that keep the dashboard recurring card live without manual cache invalidation, and compound indexes that make the data integrity constraint (preserve past expenses on template delete via SET NULL pattern) straightforward. It adds only one small dependency (~25 KB gzipped) with low blast radius (~6–9 files). The async refactor cost is a one-time, well-scoped effort. SQLite/WASM (alt-2) was rejected due to WASM initialization overhead and Web Worker complexity disproportionate to the dataset size. Supabase (alt-3) was rejected as overkill for a single-user desktop app with no cross-device sync requirement.
alternatives: alt-1: In-Memory Store with localStorage Persistence — rejected due to 5 MB storage ceiling and lack of indexed queries
alt-2: SQLite via OPFS with sql.js — rejected due to WASM initialization overhead and Web Worker complexity
alt-3: Supabase PostgreSQL Backend — rejected as overkill for single-user desktop app
alt-4: IndexedDB with Dexie.js — chosen

adrs.title: Use CSS Modules for Styling New Recurring Transaction UI
adrs.decision: Use CSS Modules with co-located .module.css files for all new recurring-transaction components. Share design tokens (colors, spacing, timing, easing) via CSS custom properties defined in a global :root stylesheet. Handle prefers-reduced-motion via @media queries in each module file.
adrs.rationale: CSS Modules is the most likely existing styling approach in a brownfield React project (CRA/Vite default). It introduces zero new dependencies, imposes no runtime cost on the Dashboard recurring card render path (satisfying the performance constraint), and provides full scoping to prevent regressions in existing Expense and Category UI. Animation timing tokens shared via CSS custom properties on :root satisfy the animation system constraint cleanly. Tailwind (alt-tailwind) was rejected because introducing it into a brownfield project that likely doesn't use it creates a dual-styling-system problem. styled-components (alt-styled-components) was rejected due to runtime CSS injection cost and bundle size increase. Inline styles (alt-inline-styles-plus-css-vars) were rejected due to inability to use pseudo-classes and media queries directly.
alternatives: alt-css-modules: CSS Modules — chosen
alt-tailwind: Tailwind CSS — rejected due to likely dual-styling-system conflict in brownfield
alt-styled-components: styled-components — rejected due to runtime cost and bundle size
alt-inline-styles-plus-css-vars: Inline Styles + CSS Custom Properties — rejected due to pseudo-class limitations

adrs.title: Use Headless UI for Accessible Interactive Primitives
adrs.decision: Install @headlessui/react as the sole new component dependency. Use its Switch (toggle), Dialog (drawer/focus trap), and Disclosure (recurrence config slide-down) primitives. All visual styling is applied using the existing CSS Modules system.
adrs.rationale: Headless UI minimizes blast radius (5–7 files, 1 dependency at ~13 KB gzipped), satisfies all accessibility constraints natively (ARIA-compliant Switch with Space/Enter, Dialog with focus trapping and return-focus), and avoids introducing a competing design language into the existing codebase. shadcn/ui (alt-shadcn-ui) was rejected because it requires Tailwind CSS which we are not adopting. Mantine (alt-mantine) was rejected due to bundle size (~40–80 KB) and ThemeProvider root-level change. MUI (alt-mui) was rejected due to largest bundle impact (~90–130 KB) and Material Design aesthetic conflict with existing custom UI.
alternatives: alt-headlessui-minimal: Headless UI — chosen
alt-shadcn-ui: shadcn/ui — rejected (requires Tailwind)
alt-mantine: Mantine UI — rejected (bundle size, root-level ThemeProvider)
alt-mui: Material UI — rejected (largest bundle, aesthetic conflict)

adrs.title: Use Centralized Query Builder (DAL) for Data Access
adrs.decision: Introduce a centralized data-access layer (DAL) as a single module (e.g., db/queries.ts) exporting named, composable, typed query functions for all entities. RecurringTemplate occurrence computation is a pure function in this module, memoizable with useMemo or Dexie liveQuery.
adrs.rationale: The query builder DAL adds minimal files (1–2), keeps occurrence computation as a pure memoizable function (satisfying the dashboard performance constraint), enforces a single place for the template-deletion integrity rule, and is fully additive to the brownfield codebase without touching existing Expense/Category hooks. It avoids the class-hierarchy overhead of the repository pattern while providing more structure than inline hooks. The repository pattern (alt-repository-pattern) was rejected as over-engineered for a 3-entity client-side app. Active Record/ORM (alt-active-record) was rejected because no ORM exists in the stack and adding one (e.g., Prisma) is disproportionate for a client-side IndexedDB app. Inline hooks (alt-minimal-inline-hooks) were rejected due to higher divergence risk as the feature evolves.
alternatives: alt-query-builder: Centralized Query Builder — chosen
alt-repository-pattern: Repository Pattern — rejected (over-engineered for 3-entity client app)
alt-active-record: Active Record via ORM — rejected (no ORM in stack, disproportionate)
alt-minimal-inline-hooks: Inline Data Access in Hooks — rejected (divergence risk)

adrs.title: Use In-Memory Memoization for Occurrence Caching
adrs.decision: Cache computed upcoming occurrences using Dexie liveQuery reactivity combined with in-memory memoization (useMemo or a plain JS Map with TTL). No external cache infrastructure is introduced.
adrs.rationale: CashPulse is a client-side, single-user desktop app. Dexie's liveQuery already provides reactive cache invalidation when RecurringTemplate data changes, making explicit cache invalidation unnecessary. For the occurrence computation itself, useMemo or a simple Map-based cache with TTL is sufficient — occurrence generation for weekly/monthly/yearly schedules is computationally trivial (<1ms for typical datasets). Framework data cache (alt-2) was rejected because the app is client-side with no server-side rendering framework. React Query (alt-3) was rejected as an unnecessary new dependency when Dexie liveQuery already provides reactivity. Redis (alt-4) was rejected as massively overkill for a single-user client-side app.
alternatives: alt-1: In-Memory Memoization — chosen
alt-2: Framework Data Cache with Revalidation Tags — rejected (no SSR framework)
alt-3: React Query Client Cache — rejected (Dexie liveQuery provides reactivity)
alt-4: Redis Distributed Cache — rejected (overkill for single-user client app)

decisions.rationale: Dexie.js over IndexedDB is the best balance of minimal blast radius (low, ~6–9 files), a single small dependency (25 KB), significantly larger storage quota than localStorage, built-in reactive queries (liveQuery) for the dashboard recurring card, and compound indexes for data integrity enforcement. It avoids WASM overhead (alt-2) and cloud complexity (alt-3) while being a meaningful upgrade over localStorage (alt-1) for query capability and storage headroom.

CSS Modules is the most likely existing styling approach in this brownfield React project. Zero new dependencies, zero runtime cost on the Dashboard render path, full scoping to prevent regressions. Design tokens shared via CSS custom properties on :root. Avoids dual-styling-system problems that Tailwind or styled-components would introduce.

Headless UI provides the exact behavioral primitives needed (Switch, Dialog, Disclosure) with full ARIA compliance and focus management, at minimal blast radius (5–7 files, 1 dependency, ~13 KB). Zero styling opinions means it integrates cleanly with the chosen CSS Modules approach. Avoids the Tailwind dependency of shadcn/ui and the bundle bloat of Mantine or MUI.

A centralized DAL (db/queries.ts) adds minimal files (1–2), keeps occurrence computation as a pure memoizable function, enforces a single place for template-deletion integrity rules, and is fully additive to the brownfield codebase. It provides more structure than inline hooks without the class-hierarchy overhead of repositories or the ORM dependency of Active Record.

In-memory memoization combined with Dexie liveQuery reactivity is sufficient for a single-user client-side app. Occurrence computation for weekly/monthly/yearly schedules is trivial (<1ms). Dexie liveQuery automatically re-triggers queries when underlying data changes, eliminating the need for explicit cache invalidation infrastructure. No SSR framework exists to leverage framework caching (alt-2), and React Query (alt-3) and Redis (alt-4) are unnecessary given Dexie's built-in reactivity.

implementationPatterns.title: Dexie.js DAL — All persistence through centralized query functions
implementationPatterns.rule: All data reads and writes for Expense, Category, and RecurringTemplate MUST go through named, typed query functions exported from the centralized DAL module (e.g., db/queries.ts). Components and hooks MUST NOT call Dexie table methods directly. Each query function accepts typed parameters and returns typed results. Use Dexie liveQuery in React hooks to subscribe to reactive query results.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-store (Dexie.js) and gap-data-access-pattern (centralized query builder). Centralizing data access ensures the template-deletion integrity rule (preserve past expenses) is enforced in one place and occurrence computation is consistently memoizable.
forbids: Direct Dexie table.get/put/delete calls from React components or page-level files

implementationPatterns.title: Recurring template deletion preserves historical expenses
implementationPatterns.rule: When deleting a RecurringTemplate, use a Dexie transaction to first SET NULL (nullify) the recurringTemplateId on all linked Expense records, then delete the template. This MUST be a single atomic transaction. The DAL deleteTemplate function is the sole place this logic lives.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-store and constraint-nfr-004 (data integrity). Past expenses must survive template deletion. Centralizing in the DAL (gap-data-access-pattern) ensures no code path can accidentally cascade-delete historical records.
implementationPatterns.example: // Inside db/queries.ts deleteTemplate — see data-access-dexie-dal example
forbids: Deleting a RecurringTemplate without first nullifying linked Expense.recurringTemplateId

implementationPatterns.title: CSS Modules with design tokens via CSS custom properties
implementationPatterns.rule: All new components MUST use co-located .module.css files for styling. Color, spacing, radius, shadow, and animation timing values MUST reference CSS custom properties defined in the global :root stylesheet (e.g., var(--color-blue-600), var(--radius-card), var(--duration-micro)). Hard-coded hex values, pixel sizes, or timing values in .module.css files are prohibited. The :root stylesheet is the single source of truth for the PRD's design system tokens.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-styling-approach (CSS Modules). Ensures visual consistency with the existing CashPulse design system and prevents token drift between new recurring-transaction UI and existing components. Replaces baseline component-tailwind-tokens-only since Tailwind is not adopted.
forbids: Hard-coded hex color values in .module.css files
Hard-coded animation durations in .module.css files
Inline style objects for static visual properties

implementationPatterns.title: Headless UI for interactive primitives (Switch, Dialog, Disclosure)
implementationPatterns.rule: All new toggle switches MUST use Headless UI Switch. All new drawers/modals MUST use Headless UI Dialog (which provides focus trapping and return-focus). All new collapsible/expandable sections MUST use Headless UI Disclosure. Visual styling is applied via CSS Modules className props. Do NOT implement custom focus-trap or toggle ARIA logic.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-component-library (Headless UI). Ensures WCAG 2.1 AA compliance for the recurrence toggle (constraint-nfr-003), Manage Recurring drawer focus trap, and recurrence config slide-down, without custom accessibility code that is error-prone to maintain.
forbids: Custom focus-trap implementations when Headless UI Dialog is available
Custom ARIA role/state management for toggle switches

implementationPatterns.title: Respect prefers-reduced-motion in all new CSS modules
implementationPatterns.rule: Every .module.css file that defines transitions or animations MUST include a @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) block that sets transition-duration: 0ms and animation: none for all animated properties. Use the CSS custom property --duration-* tokens so that a single :root override can disable all motion globally as a fallback.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from constraint-nfr-005 (animation & motion) and the PRD's accessibility section. New UI elements (recurrence config slide-down, drawer slide-in, recurring card) must respect the existing animation system and prefers-reduced-motion.

implementationPatterns.title: Validate RecurringTemplate inputs at the form boundary
implementationPatterns.rule: All RecurringTemplate form inputs (amount, frequency, startDate, endDate) MUST be validated before calling DAL mutation functions. Validation logic lives in a shared validation module (e.g., utils/validators.ts) using plain TypeScript type guards and assertion functions. Frequency MUST be one of 'weekly' | 'monthly' | 'yearly'. EndDate, if provided, MUST be after startDate. Amount MUST be > 0 and ≤ 99999.99. Invalid inputs show inline error messages below the relevant field (per assumption-det-phantom-validation-1).
implementationPatterns.rationale: Replaces baseline validation-zod-at-boundary for this client-side app. Since there is no HTTP/RPC boundary (client-side IndexedDB), validation occurs at the form submission boundary. Plain TypeScript validators avoid adding Zod as a dependency for a client-only app with 3 entities.
forbids: Passing unvalidated user input directly to DAL mutation functions

implementationPatterns.title: Toast for transient feedback, inline messages for form errors
implementationPatterns.rule: Use toast notifications (auto-dismiss after 2 seconds, bottom-center positioned) for transient success/info feedback (e.g., 'Saved!', 'Currency updated to EUR', 'Exported'). Use inline error messages below the relevant form field for validation errors. Toast component is a shared utility rendered at the app root. Do NOT use alert() or browser-native dialogs.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from assumption-det-phantom-errors-2 (toast for minor errors, inline for form errors) and the existing PRD patterns (currency change toast in section 10, save confirmation in section 5.7). Replaces baseline api-error-rfc7807 since there is no HTTP API — error handling is entirely client-side.

implementationPatterns.title: Structured console logging for client-side debugging
implementationPatterns.rule: Use a thin logging utility (e.g., utils/logger.ts) that wraps console.log/warn/error with structured JSON-like objects including a category tag, timestamp, and relevant entity IDs. Log all DAL mutations (create, update, delete) at 'info' level and all validation failures at 'warn' level. In production builds, suppress 'debug' level logs. Do NOT use bare console.log statements scattered in component files.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Replaces baseline logging-structured-pino since there is no server-side runtime. A thin client-side logger provides debuggability for the new RecurringTemplate data flows without adding a dependency. Structured format makes it easy to migrate to a real logging service if CashPulse evolves to include a backend.

implementationPatterns.title: No auth layer — single-user client-side app
implementationPatterns.rule: CashPulse is a single-user, client-side desktop app with no server or multi-tenant data. No authentication or authorization middleware is required. All data is scoped to the browser's IndexedDB origin. If multi-user or cloud sync is added in the future, this decision must be revisited and an auth ADR created.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Replaces baseline auth-middleware-required. The app has no server, no API endpoints, and no multi-user data. Adding auth infrastructure would be pure overhead with no security benefit in the current architecture.

implementationPatterns.title: Dexie liveQuery as the reactive state source for data-driven UI
implementationPatterns.rule: Dashboard cards, expense lists, and recurring template views MUST use Dexie useLiveQuery hooks to subscribe to data changes. Component-local UI state (e.g., toggle open/closed, form field values, popover visibility) uses React useState/useReducer. Do NOT duplicate Dexie data into a separate global state store (e.g., Redux, Zustand). Dexie IS the single source of truth for persisted data.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-store (Dexie liveQuery) and gap-caching-strategy (in-memory memoization). Dexie liveQuery provides automatic reactivity — when a RecurringTemplate is created, updated, or deleted, all subscribed components re-render with fresh data. This eliminates manual cache invalidation and satisfies the dashboard performance constraint. Replaces baseline state-server-only since there is no server.

implementationPatterns.title: RecurringTemplate frequency as a TypeScript string union
implementationPatterns.rule: The RecurringTemplate.frequency field MUST be typed as the union 'weekly' | 'monthly' | 'yearly'. This type is defined in a shared types file (e.g., db/types.ts) and used consistently in the Dexie schema, DAL query functions, validation module, and UI components. Do NOT use numeric enums or free-form strings.
implementationPatterns.rationale: Derived from gap-data-store and the PRD's scope exclusion of custom intervals (constraint-scope-3). A string union is self-documenting, serializes cleanly to IndexedDB, and is exhaustively checkable in switch statements for occurrence computation logic.

stackConfig.frontend: React with CSS Modules and Headless UI (@headlessui/react) for accessible interactive primitives
stackConfig.database: IndexedDB via Dexie.js (~25 KB gzipped) with liveQuery for reactive subscriptions
stackConfig.styling: CSS Modules with design tokens defined as CSS custom properties in a global :root stylesheet
stackConfig.componentLibrary: @headlessui/react for Switch, Dialog, and Disclosure primitives
